content.problemStatement:
There are **n** courses in a university being offered. These courses are numbered from 1 to **n** in the increasing order of their difficulty. For each course, you can have some courses as prerequisites. The prerequisite courses for a course should be of lower difficulty than it. You are given an array **a** of size **n**, where **a**i denotes that there should be at least **a**i prerequisite courses for i-th course. 



The university wants to estimate the efficiency of the allocation of prerequisites of courses by maximizing the number of courses that are not prerequisites for any other course. Find out what's the maximum such number of courses possible. It is guaranteed that **a**i < i, thus making sure that it is possible to allocate the prerequisites for each of the course.
content.inputFormat: The first line of the input contains an integer T denoting the number of test cases. The first line of each test case contains an integer n. The second line of each test case contains n space separated integers denoting array a.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing an integer corresponding to the maximum number of possible courses which are not prerequisite for any other course.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
For the first test case, both courses 2 and 3 can have course 1 as a prerequisite. In this case, courses 2 and 3 are not prerequisites for any course. So, we have two such courses.
For the second test case, course 2 has course 1 as a prerequisite, and course 3 has course 2 as a prerequisite. Thus only course 3 is the only course that is not the prerequisite of any other course.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a greedy approach. We start by initializing an array dp of size n, where dp[i] represents the maximum number of courses that are not prerequisites for any other course up to the i-th course. We then iterate over the array a and for each course, we try to find the maximum number of courses that are not prerequisites for any other course by considering all possible combinations of prerequisites. We update dp[i] accordingly and finally return dp[n-1] as the result.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * n^2) — where n is the number of courses in each test case and T is the number of test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(n) — where n is the number of courses in each test case.
